title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Credit Prices Decline for the Third Consecutive Week as Volumes Continue to Rise
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(26th May â€“ 1st June)
  • A total of 729,200 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 88.13% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 310.96% WoW as 2,225,367 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $51.11/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 7.93% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 1.52% in price, reaching $55.62/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 7.78% WoW; on 3rd June, the spot price stood at $45.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $48.38/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.28% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 5.77% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week saw a rise in California LCFS Type 1 trading volumes, and a fall in Type 1Â  and spot credit prices. The White House is considering a plan to address the record backlog of small refinery biofuel exemption requests under the U.S. Renewable Fuel Standard. The backlog, which includes more than 160 outstanding requests, could affect billions of dollars in tradeable renewable fuel credits. The plan under consideration would approve many 2024 exemption requests while delaying others, some dating back to 2016. This approach aims to reduce market disruptions compared to addressing all requests at once. However, no final decision has been made, and further engagement with the industry is expected.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 7.93% WoW fall, decreasing from $55.51 to $51.11 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 1.52% WoW, from $56.48 to $55.62. Type 1 trades saw 729,200 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 88.13% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a rise of 310.96%, reaching 2,225,367 credits. [caption id="attachment_597815" align="alignnone" width="900"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 7.78% WoW, reaching $47.66/MT. On 3rd June, the average spot price was $45.50/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $48.38/MT. Â  [caption id="attachment_597816" align="alignnone" width="900"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 81 trades were reported in the market, with 19 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $76.50 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 20,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.50 per credit of Type 2 reported for one transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 30,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $58.00, and the smallest volume transfers of 1,667 credits were undertaken for a price of $55.00. [caption id="attachment_597817" align="alignnone" width="902"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot price in May for LCFS Type 1 credits stood at $54.76 through the week of May 26th, and the total number of trading volumes transacted for the same period stood at 5.09 MMT. [caption id="attachment_597818" align="alignnone" width="903"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 26th May â€“ 2nd June, total VPT recorded a 9.35% decline WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices. [caption id="attachment_597819" align="alignnone" width="902"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.28% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 5.77% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

Exclusive: White House Considers Plan to Clear Record Backlog of Small Refinery Biofuel Waiver: The White House is weighing a plan to clear a record backlog of requests from small refineries for exemptions from U.S. biofuel laws, which could include approving many current applications and requesting industry input to deal with older ones, according to three sources familiar with the plans. The U.S. Renewable Fuel Standard requires refiners to blend biofuels like corn-based ethanol into the nation's fuel supply or buy renewable fuel credits, known as RINs, from those who do. Canada to Phase Out Federal Fuel Charge Framework in Four Stages: The Government of Canada will permanently eliminate the federal fuel charge framework in four orderly stages: first, as of April 1, 2025, nearly all charging provisions will be retroactively repealed (with only a railway true-up rule remaining until October 1, 2025); second, all rebate provisions will end on October 1, 2025 (except for error-correction rebates); third, registration requirements will be removed as of November 1, 2025; and finally, on April 1, 2035, the remaining definitions, administrative rules and procedural provisions will be repealed to complete the wind-down of Part 1 of the Greenhouse Gas Pollution Pricing Act. Port of Los Angeles Adopts Near-Term Clean Truck Spending Plan: Over the next three years, the Port of Los Angeles will continueÂ its investment in clean trucks and supporting infrastructure to help transition the drayage fleet serving the Port to zero-emissions (ZE) models by 2035. The Los Angeles Board of Harbor Commissioners unanimously approved the spending plan that will guide how the Port allocates its Clean Truck Fund (CTF) revenues through June 2028. Sen. Harckham, Asm. Woerner, Advocates Urge Support for Clean Fuel Standard Bill: New York State Senator Pete Harckham and Assemblymember Carrie Woerner, along with leaders from environmental and agricultural groups, labour and the Clean Fuels coalition, urged support for the Clean Fuel Standard Act of 2025 (CFS) at a press conference today held at the State Capitol. Woerner, will reduce on-road transportation sector greenhouse gas emissions, accelerate New Yorkâ€™s shift to clean energy, and help build a more affordable and equitable future for all New Yorkers. The legislation will not only reduce emissions and support working families but will also serve as a key driver of New Yorkâ€™s electric vehicle (EV) transition by incentivizing investments in EV charging infrastructure.
